--- SOURCE 1: Integrating thermal and optical models with legacy ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638

SUMMARY:
This study uses Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAV) for the reconciliation of legacy data collected from two archaeological sites Kuruçay Höyük (Burdur) and Konya Karahöyük (Konya) from Türkiye with high-precision thermal and optical modeling. We present Kuruçay Höyük and Konya Karahöyük as two case sites with different sizes, archaeological layers, and geological settings, where UAV-based aerial photogrammetry helps to generate high-resolution mappings that provide tools to increase the accuracy of legacy data and helps digital protection of the site via constructing digital 3D models. Traslaviña Beyond the basemap: multiscalar survey through aerial photogrammetry in the andes  E.W. Averett, J.M. Gordon, D.B. Counts (Eds.), Mobilizing the Past for a Digital Future, the Potential of Digital Archaeology, The Digital Press at the University of North Dakota, Grand Forks, ND (2016), pp.

Integrating thermal and optical models with legacy excavation data for spatial inventories
==========================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Gonca Dardeniz a, Orkan Özcan b, Deniz Yazıcı b

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.daach.2024.e00378](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.daach.2024.e00378 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2212054824000638&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Natural and anthropogenic factors accelerate the deterioration of ancient ruins with mudbrick structures. 
*   •Preservation of archaeological remains appears as a time-wise and budget-wise unmanageable task. 
*   •A combination of thermal and optical imagery demonstrates a comprehensive documentation of on-site legacy data on digital platforms. 
*   •Digital preservation of cultural heritage through thermal and optical models provides a powerful tool for monitoring legacy data. 
*   •Digital twins of archaeological sites make the data available worldwide for scholars, facilitating future archaeological research, especially in inaccessible regions. 

Abstract
--------

Researching at previously excavated archaeological sites comes with its own challenge set, which includes ground-truthing previously unearthed architectural features to correlate old and new trenches. This study presents a comprehensive examination technique to approach architectural features at mound-type archaeological settlements through the synergistic utilization of thermal and optical imagery obtained via remote sensing technologies. Employing an interdisciplinary approach involving various types of imagery (optical, thermal, and radar) embedded in archaeological data, this research aims to reevaluate legacy spatial data and enhance the understanding of subsurface archaeological features by exploiting the complementary advantages of both thermal and optical sensors.

This study involves the acquisition, processing, and integration of Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV)-based thermal and optical datasets to derive a complete representation of archaeological landscapes. Ground-truthing and validation exercises are incorporated to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the results. The results contribute to advancing the field of archaeological remote sensing and highlight the significance of integrating diverse sensing modalities for a comprehensive and nuanced interpretation of archaeological landscapes. The techniques exemplified with two mound-type case sites help to generate high-resolution mappings which provide tools to increase the accuracy of on-site legacy data and help digital protection of the sites by generating digital twins. This presents a less costly method than conventional photogrammetric techniques, making it feasible and advantageous for field archaeologists or researchers dealing with legacy spatial data.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000651)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000687)

Keywords
--------

Archaeological remains

Mound-type settlements

Digital twins

Mud-brick architecture

Remote sensing

Buried thermal remote sensing

Thermal models

Optical models

Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs)

Drones

1. Introduction
---------------

A recent trend in archaeology is working with legacy data of early excavations, which is spatially and temporally mostly low resolution. One of the major challenges in dealing with such information is understanding and reassessing the architectural features from previous digs. Reexamination of archaeological contexts requires excavation archives with systematic documentation, yet a significant number of early excavations (particularly late 19th and early 20th century campaigns) lack approvingly accurate plans. While gaps in documentation roughen working with legacy data, natural and anthropogenic factors add another layer to the complexity of the reconstruction of archaeological features and plans. If the archaeological site is not preserved, conserved, or under control, more remains as well as remnants of old excavation trenches disappear in time. While the duration of this decomposition process varies from site to site, digital documentation and reconstruction rise as a timely tool for; 1) gathering data from old excavations; 2) increasing the accuracy of legacy data, and 3) helping protect and share cultural heritage in digital platforms. This study uses Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAV) for the reconciliation of legacy data collected from two archaeological sites Kuruçay Höyük (Burdur) and Konya Karahöyük (Konya) from Türkiye with high-precision thermal and optical modeling.

Archaeological sites often consist of various construction materials such as wood, mud, and stone. These materials, when buried, exhibit different thermal absorption and emission properties compared to the surrounding soil matrix. This difference in thermal behavior can be detected as variations in thermal radiation, revealing the presence of buried archaeological features ([Kvamme, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib18)). Thermal sensors capture these variations as distinctive hot and cold patterns, effectively outlining the concealed structures. Consequently, the advent of thermal imaging technology has become invaluable for field archaeologists. The integration of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), has made aerial thermography a robust method for archaeological research.

Recent advancements in thermal camera technology have significantly enhanced our ability to detect subterranean remains. Modern thermal cameras, now compact and featuring increased thermal sensitivity and image resolution, facilitate real-time imaging and geotagging via GNSS, greatly benefiting researchers ([Casana et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib8)). The critical contrast between cultural or archaeological features and the surrounding soil or soil cover is accentuated through thermal imaging, particularly when conducted under optimal weather conditions—avoiding rain and wind. When combined with other geophysical data, the efficacy of thermal imaging in revealing archaeological remains is further enhanced. Continuous advancements in sensor technology, UAV platforms, and analytical software promise ongoing innovations in archaeological remote sensing using thermal imaging techniques.

In parallel, multispectral imaging has become an indispensable and transformative tool in archaeology ([Liang, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib22); [Agudo et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib4); [Calleja et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib7); [Ciccone, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib10); [Materazzi, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib23)). This sophisticated imaging technology allows for the detection of hidden artifacts, the interpretation of ancient manuscripts, and the exposure of previously unnoticed details within archaeological sites. By capturing data across multiple spectral bands -typically fewer than ten discrete wavelengths ([Liang, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib22))- multispectral imaging significantly enhances our capacity to explore and interpret the intricacies of our historical heritage.

The development and integration of portable and remote multispectral, as well as hyperspectral imaging systems, have expanded the possibilities for field applications. These advanced systems are adept at surveying large areas and identifying subsurface structures or materials based on their spectral signatures. Multispectral imaging has profoundly influenced archaeological practices by offering non-invasive methods to uncover hidden details in artifacts and sites, thereby deepening our understanding and aiding in the preservation of cultural heritage.

[Calleja et al. (2018)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib7) underscore the transformative potential of combining multispectral imaging with UAV data in archaeological research. This integration provides detailed spectral and spatial information, significantly enhancing the ability to detect, analyze, and interpret buried archaeological remains. The synergy of these cutting-edge tools marks a significant advancement in non-invasive archaeological methodologies, offering new perspectives to secure our cultural heritage in multi-layered digital media. A critical component of archaeological documentation is the precise modeling of archaeological features and artifacts. Recently, the utilization of UAVs has facilitated the analysis of archaeological remains in both two and three dimensions, contributing to the high-accuracy digitization of architectural plans. These photogrammetric approaches are rapidly supplanting traditional measurement techniques ([Gosh, 1981](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib17); [Fussell, 1982](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib16); [Marín-Buzón et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib24)) by delivering faster and more accurate results. Leveraging these technological advancements, numerous studies have been conducted for data analysis and land surveying ([Waagen, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib35)).

UAVs offer extensive alternatives for the exploration of archaeological sites and landscapes ([Lambers et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib19); [Verhoeven, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib34); [Sauerbier and Eisenbeiss, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib31); [Remondino et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib28); [Rinaudo et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib29); [Brenningmeyer et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib5); [Fernández-Hernandez et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib15); [Lang et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib20); [Stek, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib32); [Wernke et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib36); [Campana, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib6); [McMahon et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib25)). Modern fieldwork photography now captures 3D data through image-based modeling techniques, such as the Structure-from-Motion (SfM) method ([Remondino and El-Hakim, 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib27); [Lambers et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib19); [Sapirstein, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib30); [Wernke et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib36)). Studies increasingly focus on the application of camera-equipped UAVs in conjunction with photogrammetry to create high-accuracy orthophotos and spatially detailed 3D recordings of land surfaces and structures ([Brenningmeyer et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib5); [Ostrowski and Hanus, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib26); [Wernke et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib36); [Campana, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib6)).

Digital twins are detailed digital replicas of physical assets, allowing for comprehensive representation and interaction with these assets in the digital realm. Niccolucci et al. (2022) propose creating Heritage Digital Twins (HDTs) that encompass the physical aspects of cultural heritage objects and their intangible and contextual information, organized in a knowledge graph. This approach aims to support the preservation, study, and dynamic interaction with cultural heritage through advanced digital technologies, enabling improved conservation, accessibility, and engagement. This research serves as a foundational priority for advancing future studies in digital twin technologies.

The two case sites subjected to this study were selected based on their different sizes and geological settings ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#fig1)). Kuruçay Höyük is a small mound-type settlement located in southwestern Anatolia, close to a lacunal environment. The archaeological layers span neolithic and chalcolithic periods with interruptions, among which the late chalcolithic remains (ca. 3600–3300 BCE) establish a significant foundation for scholars to understand the chalcolithic period in the region. The excavations yielded architectural remains of stone and mud brick, the latter being very sensitive to climatic and human impacts. While the excavation was published in two separate monographs ([Duru, 1994](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib13), [1996](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib14)), there are some inconsistencies in geographical coordinates caused by the early documentation techniques. Additionally, the site was left open causing the fading of the architectural layout, especially with the increasing human and climate impacts. Vis-à-vis the importance of the settlement for the archaeology literature, a re-examination of legacy data and digital documentation of the remains become necessary.

![Image 11: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2212054824000638-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (3MB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2212054824000638-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (3MB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2212054824000638-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Locations of Kuruçay Höyük and Konya Karahöyük on the map of Türkiye.

The second case site investigated as a part of this study is Konya Karahöyük, which is located in the Konya plain of central Anatolia Türkiye. The mound-type site covers ca. 100 ha in a dynamically changing landscape of southcentral Anatolia ([Dardeniz, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib12)). Like Kuruçay Höyük, Konya Karahöyük also yielded architectural remains of stone and mud brick, which were left open to natural and human impacts since the beginning of the first excavations in 1953. Not much has been left in print regarding the spatial documentation of the 21 trenches excavated in various sizes (max. 50×50 m) and depths (max. 29 m) ([Alp, 1994](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib2)).

This study aims to demonstrate how thermal and optical models obtained through UAV data can be used at archaeological sites excavated long ago with limited documentation. This research intends to determine ways of documenting the level of preservation and to establish a standpoint to superimpose previous data with high-precision layouts. We present Kuruçay Höyük and Konya Karahöyük as two case sites with different sizes, archaeological layers, and geological settings, where UAV-based aerial photogrammetry helps to generate high-resolution mappings that provide tools to increase the accuracy of legacy data and helps digital protection of the site via constructing digital 3D models. This also presents a less costly method than conventional photogrammetric techniques, making it feasible and advantageous for field archaeologists or researchers dealing with legacy architectural data.

2. Methodology
--------------

Thermal imaging is the recording and measurement of heat radiation with specially designed cameras. Thermal cameras record heat or thermal infrared radiation, which is a type of electromagnetic wave in the infrared range of the electromagnetic spectrum ([Cool, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib9)). The recent inventory of remote sensing data includes anything from black-and-white photographs to multispectral images that capture wavelengths far outside the visible light spectrum ([Leisz, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib21)). Such data has the potential to obtain difficult-to-reach areas and find sub-surface features. For example, the color changes detected with thermal cameras in the soil may indicate the presence of underground features based on different heat absorption and emission characteristics. The patterns seen in melting frost and snow, or day and night could signal the location of an archaeological site or signal an archaeological feature.

The thermal camera utilized in this study underwent meticulous calibration procedures to ensure reliable radiometric calibration, crucial for accurately transforming raw thermal data into temperature values. Processing of thermal imagery was conducted using specialized software tailored for precise temperature mapping. Additionally, RTK systems enhance accuracy by providing centimeter-level precision in real-time, correcting GPS signals using data from ground-based reference stations. GNSS observations processed through RTK technology ensure each image's position accuracy at the centimeter scale. Moreover, RTK systems improve accuracy by achieving centimeter-level precision in real-time, correcting GPS signals with data from ground-based reference stations. GNSS observations processed using RTK technology ensure precise positioning of each image at the centimeter scale ([Akay et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib3)). Besides, we produced orthomosaic images, which are photogrammetric products based on SfM techniques. SfM is an innovative, efficient, and cost-effective photogrammetric approach that produces high-precision three-dimensional data. This method involves matching objects across a series of overlapping photographs taken from various angles and locations ([Akay et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#bib3)). This study incorporates UAV-IRT (Infrared thermography) based measurements in the survey to complement existing structural assessments. UAV-based IRT measurements offer utility in identifying structures that may not be readily visible, particularly those obscured by vegetation. In the context of Kuruçay Höyük and Konya Karahöyük, the UAV flight altitude is planned according to the field of view to be obtained on the ground to obtain the 3D thermal map details.

On the other hand, this study harnesses UAV-based photogrammetry, a valuable tool for mapping buried and subsurface architecture. Photos were captured from an altitude of approximately 40 m using a DJI Mavic 2 Enterprise Advanced drone, with flight planning conducted through DJI Pilot and photogrammetry processing executed using Agisoft Metashape Pro. An illustrative flowchart diagram outlining the primary procedures and details has been developed for the archaeological sites of Kuruçay Höyük and Konya Karahöyük ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#fig2)).

![Image 12: Fig. 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2212054824000638-gr2.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (830KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2212054824000638-gr2_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (830KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2212054824000638-gr2.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 2. a) Flowchart indicating the steps from data sets to products to create the inventory of Kuruçay and Konya Karahöyük, **b)** Flight features, **c)** UAV specs. Historical and digital data are provided as orthomosaic from the Republic of Türkiye Ministry of Defense General Directorate of Mapping.

Thermal and optical sensors attached to a UAV are utilized for data collection. Subsequently, the optical and thermal data are jointly analyzed to generate 3D thermographic models [**S1; S2**]. Images captured at the two case sites are then processed using Agisoft Metashape Pro software to generate both orthoimages and 3D models. 5444 photographs were captured for photogrammetric processing in Agisoft Metashape Pro from an elevation of approximately 40–100 m. After acquiring the aerial surveys, the data processing was initiated. In Agisoft Metashape Pro the initial step involved rough alignment, followed by control point definition and final alignment. Subsequently, a dense point cloud was generated, and a mesh model was created. The mesh model, digital elevation model, and orthophoto mosaics are crucial and necessary components. We generated digital elevation models using mesh models derived from both thermal and optical imagery.

One important observation is that while optical images can be directly imported into the Agisoft Metashape Pro photogrammetry software, thermal images captured by the drone require conversion due to their distinct file extensions. Thermal images in the R-JPG format, captured by the thermal sensor, are unsuitable for direct use in photogrammetry data generation. Therefore, it is crucial to begin the process by adjusting these extensions. Once the thermal data is converted into ".tif" format, it becomes suitable for alignment and subsequent cloud creation procedures. Tasks such as image calibration, histogram expansion, and image georeferencing require meticulous attention to ensure the generation of accurate orthophotos. In summary, raw images obtained from UAV thermal data are extracted as 16-bit TIFF files with manufacturer-based radiometric calibration ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#fig2)a).

Misalignment considerations in the context of processing captured images relate to discrepancies or inaccuracies in the spatial alignment of individual images forming a dataset. These misalignments can arise due to various factors such as camera motion during image capture, changes in lighting conditions, lens distortions, or errors in the georeferencing process. Addressing misalignments is crucial for ensuring the accuracy and reliability of subsequent image processing steps, such as feature extraction, 3D reconstruction, or orthophoto generation. Techniques for mitigating misalignments include robust image registration algorithms, careful calibration of cameras and sensors, and the use of ground control points or reference markers for georeferencing purposes. Regular quality checks and adjustments during image processing can help minimize such issues and improve the overall quality of the final outputs. Consistent with the workflow delineated in [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212054824000638#fig2), the resulting model was fashioned without evident gaps or alignment-related anomalies. It is generated at a maximum of 0.81 cm/pixels and 13.09 cm/pixels in both mounds. The landscape and soil conditions in Kuruçay Höy

## Article contents

# Archaeological Aerial Thermography in Theory and Practice

Published online by Cambridge University Press: 
**18 September 2017**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)
![](/core/page-component/img/save-pdf-icon.080470e.svg)
![](/core/page-component/img/pdf-download-icon.c7fb40c.svg)
![](/core/page-component/img/pdf-download-icon.c7fb40c.svg)
![](/core/page-component/img/dropbox-icon.3d57046.svg)
![](/core/page-component/img/google-drive-icon.a50193b.svg)
![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)
![](/core/page-component/img/close-icon.194b28a.svg)
![](/core/page-component/img/share-icon.cbcfad8.svg)
![](/core/page-component/img/close-icon.194b28a.svg)

![](/core/page-component/img/rights-icon.d4a677c.svg)

## Abstract

While a long history of experimental data shows that aerial thermal images can reveal a wide range of both surface and subsurface archaeological features, technological hurdles have largely prevented more widespread use of this promising prospecting method. However, recent advances in the sophistication of thermal cameras, the reliability of commercial drones, and the growing power of photogrammetric software packages are revolutionizing archaeologists' ability to collect, process, and analyze aerial thermal imagery. This paper provides an overview of the theory behind aerial thermography in archaeology, as well as a discussion of an emerging set of methods developed by the authors for undertaking successful surveys. Summarizing investigations at archaeological sites in North America, the Mediterranean, and the Near East, our results illustrate some contexts in which aerial thermography is very effective, as well as cases in which ground cover, soil composition, or the depth and character of archaeological features present challenges. In addition, we highlight novel approaches for filtering out noise caused by vegetation, as well as methods for improving feature visibility using radiometric thermal imagery.

Si bien una larga historia de datos experimentales muestra que las imágenes térmicas aéreas pueden ser utilizadas para detectar una amplia gama de rasgos arqueológicos tanto superficiales como subsuperficiales, los obstáculos tecnológicos han en gran parte impedido la adopción generalizada de este prometedor método de prospección. Sin embargo, los recientes avances en la sofisticación de las cámaras térmicas, la fiabilidad de los drones comerciales y el creciente poder de los paquetes de software fotogramétricos han puesto al alcance de los arqueólogos la capacidad de recopilar, procesar y analizar imágenes térmicas aéreas. Este artículo ofrece una visión general de la teoría detrás de la aplicación de la termografía aérea en la arqueología, así como una discusión de un conjunto emergente de métodos desarrollados por los autores para llevar a cabo prospecciones remotas. Se resumen las investigaciones realizadas en sitios arqueológicos de Norteamérica, el Mediterráneo y el Cercano Oriente, cuyos resultados ilustran casos en los que la termografía aérea es muy eficaz, así como contextos en los que la cubierta vegetal, la composición del suelo o la profundidad y características específicas de los rasgos arqueológicos presentan desafíos. Destacamos nuevos avances para filtrar el “ruido” causado por la vegetación y métodos para mejorar la visibilidad de los rasgos arqueológicos utilizando imágenes térmicas radiométricas.

## Information

Since the 1970s, archaeologists have known that thermal infrared images have the potential to reveal archaeological features ranging from buried architecture to concentrations of surface artifacts and cultural landscape elements such as roads, fields, or earthworks. Despite a long history of experimental research in archaeological aerial thermal imaging (e.g., Bellerby et al. [Reference Bellerby, Noel and Branigan1990](#ref001); Buck et al. [Reference Buck, Sabol and Gillespie2003](#ref003); Giardino and Haley [Reference Giardino, Haley and Johnson2006](#ref015); Haley et al. [Reference Haley, Johnson and Stallings2002](#ref018); Lundén [Reference Lundén1985](#ref033); Périsset and Tabbagh [Reference Périsset and Tabbagh1981](#ref035); Scollar et al. [Reference Scollar, Tabbagh, Hessse and Herzog1990](#ref039):593–611; Tabbagh [Reference Tabbagh1977](#ref048), [Reference Tabbagh1979](#ref049)), applications of the technology are scarce due to the difficulty and expense of deploying advanced thermal sensors on aircraft (e.g., Challis et al. [Reference Challis, Kincey and Howard2009](#ref006); Hailey [Reference Hailey2005](#ref017); Kvamme [Reference Kvamme2006a](#ref029), [Reference Kvamme2008](#ref031); Scollar et al. [Reference Scollar, Tabbagh, Hessse and Herzog1990](#ref039):614–18; Sever and Wagner [Reference Sever, Wagner and Trombold1991](#ref041); Sheets and Sever [Reference Sheets, Sever and Trombold1991](#ref042)).

Recent advances in the sophistication of thermal cameras, the reliability of commercial drones, and the growing power of photogrammetric software packages collectively have revolutionized archaeologists’ ability to collect, process, and analyze aerial thermal imagery (Casana et al. [Reference Casana, Kantner, Wiewel and Cothren2014](#ref005); Poirier et al. [Reference Poirier, Hautefeuille and Calastrenc2013](#ref036); Vasterling and Meyer [Reference Vasterling and Meyer2013](#ref052)). Aerial thermography now enables us to prospect for ancient cultural features across large areas at low cost, offering potentially transformative perspectives on the archaeological landscape. With these opportunities, there are also new challenges, ranging from the most suitable equipment to use, when, where, and how to collect imagery, and best practices for processing and interpretation of results. This paper offers archaeologists interested in aerial thermography a straightforward guide to the basic principles, suitable technology options, and key survey strategies, highlighting results from several different archaeological sites.

## BACKGROUND

The principles behind aerial thermographic imaging in archaeology are relatively simple: due to differences in composition, density, and moisture content, materials on and below the ground surface absorb, emit, transmit, and reflect thermal infrared radiation at different rates ([Figure 1](#fig001)). A wide range of archaeological features should all theoretically be visible in a thermal image if (1) there is a sufficient contrast in the thermal properties of archaeological features and the soil matrix or ground cover; (2) the archaeological materials are close enough to the surface to be affected by heat flux; and (3) the image is acquired at a time when such differences are pronounced. Put simply, a buried stone wall may heat and cool at a different rate than surrounding soil, and at the right time of day or night, this difference is potentially visible in a thermal image of the surface.

![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAMLCwgAAACH5BAAAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAICRAEAOw==)

Figure 1. Hypothetical illustration of the relative thermal radiance of dry rocks and soil versus water or saturated soil over a diurnal cycle (after Kuenzer and Dech [Reference Kuenzer, Dech, Kuenzer and Dech2013](#ref028)). Due to these differences, a wide range of archaeological features are potentially resolvable in thermal imagery, including (a) surface artifact concentrations; (b) pits, ditches, or earthworks; (c) subsurface architecture; and (d) features with topographic expression.

As with any geophysical prospection method, researchers should carefully consider the thermal properties of the features or objects they are trying to image versus the properties of the surrounding or overlying matrix, materials, or what Kvamme ([Reference Kvamme and Johnson2006b](#ref030)) describes as the signal versus the noise. Instances in which there is a large difference in thermal properties between target features and the background will be much more likely to produce good results. Detailed discussions of thermal infrared remote sensing (Kuenzer and Dech [Reference Kuenzer, Dech, Kuenzer and Dech2013](#ref028)) and its theoretical application to archaeology (Scollar et al. [Reference Scollar, Tabbagh, Hessse and Herzog1990](#ref039)) are available, but below we review a few basic thermal properties that are most relevant to consider for archaeological applications:

**Thermal conductivity** describes the ability of a material to transfer heat (or thermal infrared energy) and is measured in watts per meter-kelvin (W/mK). Soils or sediments with relatively high thermal conductivity, such as wet sand (2.0–4.0 W/mK), will result in deeper transfer of thermal energy below the surface, while those with low conductivity such as dry clay-rich soil (0.15–0.18 W/mK) will inhibit the transfer of heat.

**Volumetric heat capacity** describes the amount of thermal energy that a given volume of material must absorb to increase its temperature by one degree, a property that is related to its density and composition. Differences in volumetric heat capacity explain, in part, why a stone will likely be hotter than a loose soil, even under identical temperature and light conditions.

**Thermal inertia** is a property that describes the rate of heat transfer of different materials; quantitatively, it is the square root of a material's thermal conductivity and its volumetric heat capacity. For the purposes of thermal surveys, thermal inertia is a key variable, as the differing rates of temperature change of materials, features, or soils across a period of heat flux will largely determine the optimal time to collect imagery, as well as whether particular features will be visible. For example, water has a relatively high thermal inertia, such that the temperature of wet soils will remain more constant as compared to dry soils ([Figure 1](#fig001)).

**Thermal emissivity** describes how effectively a material emits or reflects thermal radiation. It is measured as a ratio between thermal radiation from the surface of an object and radiation from an ideal matte black surface (0 to 1 scale). If some surface objects, such as lithics or ceramics, for example, have emissivity values substantially different than the ground, they may be visible in thermal imagery. In addition, because metals with unoxidized or unpainted surfaces tend to have very low emissivity, they can make good ground control points, as they will typically appear nearly black in thermal images regardless of the ambient or ground temperature.

With a basic understanding of these key variables, we can make a few generalizations about the kinds of archaeological features that are potentially resolvable in thermal imagery. Surface artifact scatters may be detectable in a thermal image if they are sufficiently distinct in thermal properties from surrounding soils or ground cover ([Figure 1](#fig001)a). Experimental data (Buck et al. [Reference Buck, Sabol and Gillespie2003](#ref003)) show that even in imagery of relatively coarse spatial resolution, some common materials, such as obsidian, are detectable in thermal imagery at rather low densities. With the ability to collect thermal imagery in which the ground-sampling distance of the image approaches the dimensions of artifacts, it may be possible to distinguish differences in surface artifact density.

Topographic features of all varieties are likely to be apparent in thermal imagery, as they have different thermal properties than otherwise flat ground; a raised berm, for example, will heat and cool at a faster rate than the surrounding ground ([Figure 1](#fig001)d). In addition, topographic features will heat differentially at dawn when the first light of the sun strikes them obliquely, and these differences are readily apparent in thermal imagery (Casana et al. [Reference Casana, Kantner, Wiewel and Cothren2014](#ref005); Scollar et al. [Reference Scollar, Tabbagh, Hessse and Herzog1990](#ref039)).

Pits, ditches, field boundaries, roadways, and other similar features at or near the modern surface are likely to be apparent in thermal imagery even if they have no topographic expression, as they typically possess differences in soil composition and moisture retention, both of which affect their thermal properties ([Figure 1](#fig001)b). For example, the earth lodges at the site of Double Ditch, North Dakota, likely create moisture traps that increase their visibility in Kvamme's ([Reference Kvamme2008](#ref031)) survey of the site, and a similar process may account for the appearance of a possible Great Kiva, a round, semisubterranean ceremonial structure detected in our own work at Blue J (Casana et al. [Reference Casana, Kantner, Wiewel and Cothren2014](#ref005)).

Finally, thermal imagery can detect subsurface architectural remains if they are constructed of materials with sufficiently different thermal properties compared to the soil matrix in which they are buried ([Figure 1](#fig001)c). The depth at which subsurface archaeological features can be resolved varies considerably, depending on the thermal properties of the soil, the thickness and composition of ground cover, and the magnitude and rate of heat flux. Experimental data suggest that diurnal heat flux will impact at least the upper 50 cm of the soil profile (Périsset [Reference Périsset1980](#ref034)), but differences in thermal conductivity of soils can vary by a factor of 20. At the same time, longer-term transient heat flux across a period of several days may reveal features at considerably greater depth, potentially down to several meters below the surface (Scollar et al. [Reference Scollar, Tabbagh, Hessse and Herzog1990](#ref039): 597–600).

## TECHNOLOGY

### Thermal Cameras

When the first archaeological aerial thermal surveys were conducted in the 1970s and 1980s, researchers required a large, liquid nitrogen-cooled scanning radiometer that recorded imagery on long rolls of film. Thermal camera technology has advanced considerably in recent years, with modern, uncooled camera systems measuring a few centimeters in size, with increased thermal sensitivity (< 0.05°K) and image resolution (640 × 512 pixels). Some manufacturers have begun producing thermal cameras specifically built for integration with drones, such as the FLIR Vue series and the DRS Tamarisk, enabling users to view aerial thermal images in real time, as well as facilitating GNSS geotagging of imagery as it is collected. Most thermal cameras employ some form of automatic gain control in which the camera's software will assess the minimum and maximum values in a given scene and then output a processed 8-bit video feed that stretches the values in the scene to fit a constant range of color values. Automatic gain control is helpful to quickly assess relative thermal values in a given image, but it reduces the dynamic range of values the camera is capable of discriminating to 256, likely obscuring subtle differences among features. A new generation of more sophisticated radiometric cameras like the FLIR Vue Pro R, as well as after-market components such as TeAx's ThermalCapture system, now enable users to capture raw, full-spectrum thermal imagery, enabling more advanced analyses. Finally, for aerial thermography, a shorter focal length lens (9mm or 13mm) is generally preferable, as it will increase the field of view, and therefore the footprint that can be covered at a given elevation, with the caveat that it will also increase image distortion, producing greater parallax at image edges.

### Aerial Platforms

Recent years have seen remarkable advances in drone technology, quickly moving from a novelty to a standard part of the archaeological tool kit (e.g., Chiabrando et al. [Reference Chiabrando, Nex, Piatti and Rinaudo2011](#ref007); Hill [Reference Hill2013](#ref021); Hill et al. [Reference Hill, Rowan and Kersel2014](#ref022); Seitz and Altenbach [Reference Seitz and Altenbach2011](#ref040); Smith et al. [Reference Smith, Passone, Al-Said, Al-Farhan and Levy2014](#ref043); Sonnemann et al. [Reference Sonnemann, Ulloa Hung and Hofman2016](#ref044); Thomas [Reference Thomas2016](#ref050); Verhoeven and Docter [Reference Verhoeven and Docter2013](#ref054); Wernke et al. [Reference Wernke, Adams and Hooten2014](#ref059)). Small, inexpensive copter-type drones, such as the Phantom 3 and 4 or its many competitors, are increasingly easy to fly, with auto takeoff and landing functions and seamless integration with tablet or smartphone navigation. However, with sleeker engineering and out-of-the-box flight capability, some platforms make it difficult to use camera systems other than those supplied by manufacturers. In planning thermal surveys, key factors to consider are whether a given aerial platform will enable full integration with a thermal camera, so that imagery can be viewed in real time and geotagged. Our most recent flights have been undertaken using the now discontinued 3DR Solo quadcopter that utilizes a custom gimble to enable the FLIR Vue camera line (including the Vue Pro R) to operate with the same ease as a standard color camera. It is a near certainty that drone technology will continue to see improvements in coming years, with longer flight times, more intuitive controls, and more seamless integration with GIS-based mapping applications. However, it is also worth noting that, despite the power and efficiency of copter-type drones for archaeological thermography, there are parts of the world where drones are illegal to deploy or difficult to acquire. In these instances, it is also possible to collect thermal imagery on more conventional balloon or kite rigs (Verhoeven [Reference Verhoeven2009](#ref053); Wells [Reference Wells2011](#ref058)) or full-scale aircraft.

### Software

Along with small, uncooled thermal cameras and reliable, commercial drones, aerial thermography would not be feasible on a large scale without modern photogrammetric software packages such as Agisoft's PhotoScan Pro and Pix4D mapper, which are increasingly transforming archaeological documentation more broadly (e.g., De Reu et al. [Reference De Reu, Plets, Verhoeven, De Smedt, Bats, Cherretté, De Maeyer, Deconynck, Herremans, Laloo, Van Meirvenne and De Clercq2013](#ref010); Koenig et al. [Reference Koenig, Willis and Black2017](#ref027); Porter et al. [Reference Porter, Roussel and Soressi2016](#ref037); Stek [Reference Stek2016](#ref046); Verhoeven, Doneus, et al. [Reference Verhoeven, Doneus, Briese and Vermeulen2012](#ref055); Verhoeven, Taelman, et al. [Reference Verhoeven, Taelman and Vermeulen2012](#ref056)). These software packages enable much of the mapping process to be accomplished with relative ease thanks to automatic image orientation (or what is now commonly termed “image alignment”) and powerful terrain and orthoimage generation capabilities. If you are using a conventional thermal camera that outputs a video feed, it is possible to simply extract images at regular intervals through the video file using freeware such as ImageGrab and then import these images into Agisoft PhotoScan or other photogrammetric software (with the added step of manually inputting camera pixel size and focal length). However, thermal images tend to be very small (640 × 512 pixels at the most), so images over fairly homogeneous terrain, especially with 8-bit color resolution, may lack sufficient variability to enable automated alignment. Collecting imagery from a higher altitude, and thus with a larger footprint (albeit at a lower ground resolution) can help to create images with more features, but can also produce wildly differing contrast in adjacent images due to the automatic gain control function. We have sometimes been successful in applying image-processing techniques such as contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE) to extracted thermal frames prior to processing, which makes individual frames more uniform and facilitates automatic alignment in some instances. Alternatively, radiometric thermal cameras that collect full-spectrum imagery have much more information in each image and don't compress values based on the overall scene, thus offering software more opportunities to find features to anchor alignment.

## METHODS

### Timing of Surveys

The timing of an aerial thermal survey is among the most important issues in determining its success, as imagery collected just an hour apart is often strikingly different in how well it reveals archaeological features (Casana et al. [Reference Casana, Kantner, Wiewel and Cothren2014](#ref005)). Unfortunately, predicting the optimal time for data collection, either seasonally or in the course of a particular diurnal cycle, remains challenging owing to the myriad factors that can influence the thermal properties of specific features (Scollar et al. [Reference Scollar, Tabbagh, Hessse and Herzog1990](#ref039):593–609). In general, if our goal is to document buried architecture or surface features such as pits, ditches, or earthworks, based on diurnal heat flux, it is probably best to collect thermal imagery between sunset and sunrise. During daylight hours, a large fraction of thermal infrared radiation imaged by a camera is derived from reflected sunlight (Kuenzer and Dech [Reference Kuenzer, Dech, Kuenzer and Dech2013](#ref028)), which may mask the more subtle differences in emitted radiation. Typically, soon after sunset when features on and below the ground are warmest, vegetation will create the most noise and differences among features will be less pronounced, but as the ground cools over the course of the night, differences in thermal inertia among features or soils will result in greater difference in their appearance in a thermal image ([Figure 1](#fig001)). The optimal moment for the collection of imagery will vary depending on the properties of the features in question, the absolute difference in diurnal heat flux, the rate of temperature change on a given day, and other environmental variables such as clouds, rain, and wind speed (Haley et al. [Reference Haley, Johnson and Stallings2002](#ref018); Sever and Wagner [Reference Sever, Wagner and Trombold1991](#ref041)). For this reason, we typically collect thermal imagery at several points during the course of a night. It is also possible to detect features on the basis of longer-term transient heat flux, when there is significant change in the daily mean temperature over a period of several days or weeks, and Scollar and coauthors ([Reference Scollar, Tabbagh, Hessse and Herzog1990](#ref039): 597–600) argue that this approach should theoretically produce better results. However, our dataset regarding transient versus diurnal heat flux in archaeological prospecting is so limited that it remains difficult to assess their relative importance.

Alternatively, if our goal is to document archaeological features with topographic expression, such as raised fields, walls, or earthen mounds, it is best to collect imagery around sunrise. Topographic features will heat differentially under raking sunlight, and thermal imagery collected at this time can therefore reveal such features with great clarity (Casana et al. [Reference Casana, Kantner, Wiewel and Cothren2014](#ref005)). In addition, surface artifacts or other cultural features on the ground surface may have significant differences in thermal emissivity and should similarly be identifiable in daylight thermal imagery, but, again, there are few published examples (see Buck et al. [Reference Buck, Sabol and Gillespie2003](#ref003)).

### Thermal Survey Strategies

As with any aerial survey, when planning a thermal survey, researchers should carefully consider the desired results in terms of the spatial resolution of the final dataset and the overall size of the survey area ([Figure 2](#fig002)). We use a spreadsheet with linked fields in order to calculate ground resolution, image footprint size, image resolution, flight time, speed, and other variables, which is helpful as most automatic flight planning software does not enable users to change camera parameters to account for the field of view and pixel size of thermal cameras. For example, flying at 30 m elevation with our 9mm fixed lens on the FLIR Vue Pro (640 × 512 pixels), we collect images that cover 29 × 36 m on the ground and that have 5.7 cm resolution at nadir, thus approximating high-resolution archaeo-geophysical data. To achieve different image resolution or footprint size, operators can simply fly at higher or lower elevation.

![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAMLCwgAAACH5BAAAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAICRAEAOw==)

Figure 2. Diagram illustrating key variables in planning drone surveys.

One of the main limiting factors in covering larger areas using thermal imaging is the relatively short flight time possible with most copter-type drones, often 15–20 minutes at the most. Moreover, because thermal camera sensors heat and cool progressively in response to received radiation, images can easily become blurred if the camera is moving too quickly during flight, so drone speed must be limited during thermal data collection, more than with collection of color imagery. Survey speed can be increased in relation to the camera's elevation above the ground. As a rule of thumb, we opt for speeds not exceeding 3–4 m/s at 30-m elevation, and 5–6 m/s at 60 m. Because thermal imagery collection is so time sensitive, it is essential to plan surveys efficiently if a large area is being surveyed to maximize coverage.

In order to facilitate automated alignment of thermal images in photogrammetric software, it is critical to have at least 70–80% overlap between adjacent images, generally meaning images should be collected or sampled at 1–2 seconds, depending on speed. The distance between individual survey transects is another key consideration. While it is possible to achieve good results in image processing with as little as 60% sidelap between adjacent transects, it is better to target sidelap coverage of 70–80% to ensure automatic alignment does not fail, particularly when surveying relatively homogeneous terrain.

While it is possible to collect usable thermal imagery by flying drones manually above archaeological sites, the need to collect imagery quickly, at night, and with greater precision than is necessary for typical visible light photography means that it is much better to use mission-planning software to program autonomous flights. This approach will also ensure that the same area can be imaged at repeated intervals with the same coverage and resolution. There are numerous manufacturer and third-party mission planning apps, but our most recent surveys have been conducted using Pix4D Capture, a fairly flexible and reliable software app that can integrate with a variety of commercial drones.

To aid in accurate georeferencing of imagery, it is generally necessary to place a series of ground control points throughout a survey area and to collect accurate XYZ coordinates for these points. We use ground control points made of sheet aluminum flashing, as it is inexpensive and durable in field conditions ([Figure 3](#fig003)a). Because of its very low thermal emissivity (0.02 W/mK), nonanodized, unpainted aluminum sheeting appears as nearly black in thermal imagery, and thus can be readily recognized in images ([Figure 3](#fig003)b–c). enriched_points.expanded_content: Thermal infrared imaging captures temperature variations, revealing subsurface archaeological features by detecting differences in thermal properties between buried structures and surrounding soil.
Analyzing temperature differences allows archaeologists to identify buried features, as distinct thermal signatures emerge from materials like mudbrick and stone compared to the soil matrix.
Thermal imaging effectively reveals subsurface structures by highlighting anomalies, enabling the mapping of buried walls and foundations linked to Bronze Age settlements.
